items.title: Medical test with a rare disease
items.scenario: A disease affects 1 in 1,000 people (prior P(diseased)=0.001). A test has 99% sensitivity and 99% specificity. A patient tests positive—what is P(diseased|+)?
items.explanation: The posterior is much lower than 99% because the prior is tiny: most positives are false positives. Bayes combines the strong likelihood of a positive test with the rare-diseased prior, yielding roughly ~9% posterior probability in the standard arithmetic—intuition beats trusting sensitivity alone.

items.title: Coin bias after 8 heads in 10 flips
items.scenario: You start with a uniform prior on coin bias θ ~ Beta(1,1). You observe 8 heads in 10 flips (Bernoulli likelihood).
items.explanation: The posterior is Beta(9,3), peaked near 0.75 but still spread out—8/10 data pull the belief upward from the flat prior without claiming certainty. More flips would tighten the posterior.

items.title: Ridge as a Gaussian prior on weights
items.scenario: Linear regression with Gaussian noise and independent N(0, τ²) priors on each coefficient β_j.
items.explanation: MAP estimation under this prior equals Ridge regression with penalty λ ∝ 1/τ². A tighter prior (small τ) shrinks coefficients more—posterior mean balances fit to data with prior belief that weights are small.
